agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Proposed change to auto-delete delivery records after 90 days, down from 7-year retention, framed as infrastructure optimization. The platform operates in EU and UK markets serving both B2C consumers and B2B logistics companies who depend on delivery data for their own compliance reporting. This is NOT a simple infrastructure change — delivery records are legal and financial artifacts, not just operational data.","bounded_context":"Delivery Record Management / Data Retention Policy — intersects Legal Compliance, Financial Reporting, Customer Service, and B2B Partner obligations.","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Delivery Record","definition":"The complete data artifact for a completed delivery, including proof of delivery, timestamps, recipient details, item descriptions, and financial data. Serves as a legal document for tax, customs, insurance, and dispute resolution purposes."},{"term":"Data Retention Policy","definition":"The rules governing how long business records are stored before disposal. In regulated markets, retention periods are dictated by law, not by storage cost preferences."},{"term":"Proof of Delivery (POD)","definition":"Evidence that a shipment was delivered to the correct recipient. Required for dispute resolution, insurance claims, and B2B contractual obligations."},{"term":"B2B Compliance Reporting","definition":"The use of delivery data by business customers for their own regulatory filings, tax returns, audit trails, and supply chain compliance."},{"term":"Right to Erasure (GDPR Art. 17)","definition":"A data subject's right to request deletion of personal data, subject to exemptions including legal obligation and legitimate business interest for record-keeping."},{"term":"Statutory Retention Period","definition":"The legally mandated minimum period for which certain business records must be preserved, varying by jurisdiction and record type."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"Delivery records that constitute tax-relevant commercial documents must be retained for the minimum statutory period required by applicable law (typically 6-10 years in EU/UK jurisdictions).","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Violating statutory retention periods exposes the company to regulatory fines, audit failures, and legal liability. This is a legal constraint, not a business policy.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN a delivery record in a jurisdiction with a 6-year statutory retention period WHEN the record is 5 years old THEN the record MUST still exist and be retrievable."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Delivery records required for ongoing or potential legal disputes must not be deleted while the limitation period for claims is still open.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Destroying evidence during an open or potential claim window constitutes spoliation and creates severe legal liability.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN a delivery record WHEN the statute of limitations for contract/consumer claims has not yet expired THEN the record MUST NOT be auto-deleted."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"B2B customers who rely on delivery data for their own compliance reporting must retain access to that data for the duration of their own statutory obligations.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a contractual and commercial obligation that could theoretically be renegotiated, but unilateral removal would breach existing contracts and destroy customer trust.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN a B2B customer with a 7-year tax audit window WHEN they request delivery records from 3 years ago THEN those records MUST be available."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"Any data retention policy change must be reviewed and approved by Legal and Compliance before implementation.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Engineering teams cannot unilaterally determine legal retention requirements. Deletion of legally required records is irreversible.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN a proposed retention policy change WHEN no legal review has been completed THEN the change MUST NOT be deployed."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"GDPR does not mandate deletion of delivery records at 90 days; it permits retention where there is a legal obligation or legitimate business interest, and requires deletion only when no lawful basis for processing exists.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Misapplying GDPR to justify premature deletion of legally required records would violate both GDPR compliance (lawful basis for retention exists) and statutory record-keeping laws simultaneously.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN a delivery record with a lawful retention basis (tax, legal obligation) WHEN the record is older than 90 days THEN the system MUST NOT delete it under a GDPR justification."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A delivery record exists for an EU or UK transaction","when":"The auto-deletion process evaluates the record","then":"The record MUST NOT be deleted if it is within the statutory retention period for tax records in the applicable jurisdiction (e.g., 6 years UK, 10 years Germany)"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"A B2B customer has contractual access to delivery data for compliance reporting","when":"The retention policy is applied","then":"Records covered by B2B contractual obligations MUST be retained for the full contractual period, regardless of the new default policy"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"A delivery record is subject to an open dispute, claim, or investigation","when":"The auto-deletion process evaluates the record","then":"The record MUST be placed on legal hold and excluded from auto-deletion entirely"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"The proposed 90-day retention policy","when":"Legal and Compliance have NOT reviewed and approved the change","then":"The change MUST NOT be deployed to any environment containing real customer data"},{"id":"AC-5","given":"A delivery record contains personal data and has passed ALL applicable statutory retention periods, contractual obligations, and claim windows","when":"The auto-deletion process evaluates the record","then":"The record SHOULD be deleted or anonymized in compliance with GDPR data minimization principles"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"German B2B customer requires 10-year retention for Handelsgesetzbuch (HGB) compliance but the platform deletes at 90 days","expected_behavior":"Record must be retained for 10 years. Deletion would cause the customer to fail a tax audit, creating contractual liability for the platform."},{"scenario":"Consumer files a product liability claim 18 months after delivery (well within the 6-year UK limitation period)","expected_behavior":"Delivery record must exist to defend/resolve the claim. If auto-deleted at 90 days, the platform loses critical evidence."},{"scenario":"HMRC or EU tax authority audits the platform 4 years after transactions occurred","expected_behavior":"All delivery records for the audit period must be producible. Failure to produce records results in penalties and presumption against the company."},{"scenario":"A cross-border delivery (e.g., France to Germany) is subject to two different jurisdictions' retention requirements","expected_behavior":"The longest applicable retention period must govern. The system must track per-record jurisdiction, not apply a blanket policy."},{"scenario":"GDPR data subject access request (DSAR) for a delivery record that was auto-deleted at 90 days but should have been retained for tax purposes","expected_behavior":"This creates a compliance paradox — the record should have existed. Premature deletion means the platform violated BOTH tax law (by deleting) AND potentially GDPR (by losing the ability to respond to the DSAR accurately)."},{"scenario":"Insurance claim for lost/damaged goods filed 6 months after delivery","expected_behavior":"Proof of delivery record must exist to process the insurance claim. Auto-deletion at 90 days destroys the evidence needed."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"SEVERE. Potential regulatory fines under EU/UK tax law for failure to retain statutory records. GDPR fines up to 4% of global turnover if the policy is misapplied. Loss of B2B customers whose compliance depends on data access. Liability for B2B customers' audit failures caused by missing records. Storage cost savings are trivial compared to a single regulatory action.","operational":"HIGH. Irreversible data loss — once deleted, records cannot be recovered. Every downstream process that depends on historical delivery data (analytics, dispute resolution, audit response, insurance claims) breaks. Requires per-jurisdiction retention logic, not a single blanket policy.","user":"CRITICAL for B2B customers who use delivery data for their own compliance reporting — this change would break their audit trail without warning. B2C consumers lose ability to reference past deliveries for disputes, returns, or warranty claims beyond 90 days.","risk":"EXTREME. This change as proposed would simultaneously violate: (1) EU/UK tax record retention laws, (2) existing B2B contractual obligations, (3) consumer protection claim windows, (4) potential insurance and liability evidence preservation requirements. The framing as 'no business logic changes' is dangerously incorrect — this is a fundamental business logic change with legal implications.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"B2B Logistics Customers","impact":"Loss of access to delivery data needed for their own tax filings, customs compliance, and audit trails. Could cause THEM to fail regulatory audits. High churn risk and potential breach of contract claims."},{"group":"B2C Consumers","impact":"Loss of ability to reference delivery records for disputes, warranty claims, returns, or proof of purchase beyond 90 days. Degraded customer experience and trust."},{"group":"Finance / Tax Team","impact":"Unable to produce records required for tax audits. Platform exposed to penalties, back-assessments, and adverse audit findings."},{"group":"Legal / Compliance Team","impact":"Loss of evidence for dispute resolution, litigation defense, and regulatory responses. Spoliation risk in active or future claims."},{"group":"Insurance Partners","impact":"Unable to process claims that require proof of delivery for incidents discovered after 90 days."},{"group":"Engineering Team","impact":"Proposed solution is too simplistic. Actual implementation requires jurisdiction-aware, obligation-aware retention logic — significantly more complex than a blanket TTL."}]},"confidence":{"level":40,"reasoning":"Confidence is very low because critical legal and regulatory context has NOT been provided by anyone in this scenario. The specific statutory retention periods for each EU/UK jurisdiction the platform operates in have not been confirmed by legal counsel. B2B contractual obligations regarding data access have not been reviewed. Without legal sign-off, any retention period shorter than the current 7 years carries unquantified regulatory risk. The engineering team's characterization as 'no business logic changes' is factually incorrect, further reducing confidence that the full implications have been considered.","high_confidence_areas":["EU/UK jurisdictions have statutory retention requirements for commercial records that exceed 90 days","B2B customers depend on delivery data for their own compliance","Auto-deletion of legally required records is irreversible and creates regulatory liability","This is NOT merely an infrastructure change — it is a business logic and legal compliance change"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact statutory retention periods per jurisdiction the platform operates in — need legal review","Specific B2B contractual terms regarding data access and retention obligations","Whether the platform has any existing legal holds or pending disputes that would be affected","Customs and cross-border regulatory requirements for international deliveries","Insurance policy requirements for proof of delivery retention"],"assumptions":["The platform handles tax-relevant commercial transactions subject to EU/UK record-keeping laws","B2B customers have contractual expectations of data availability based on the current 7-year policy","No legal review of the proposed change has been conducted","The 7-year retention period was originally set to comply with statutory requirements, not arbitrarily"]},"escalations":[{"type":"missing_context","description":"No legal or regulatory review has been provided for this proposed change. The specific statutory retention periods for delivery records in each EU/UK jurisdiction the platform operates in are unknown. Without this, we cannot determine any safe retention period shorter than the current 7 years.","affected_stakeholders":["Legal/Compliance Team","Finance/Tax Team","B2B Customers","Engineering Team"],"options":["Halt the proposal until Legal provides jurisdiction-specific retention requirements","Proceed with 90-day deletion (UNACCEPTABLE — near-certain regulatory violation)","Implement tiered retention: anonymize non-essential PII at 90 days but retain legally required record fields for the full statutory period"],"recommendation":"HALT. Do not proceed until Legal and Compliance have reviewed and provided jurisdiction-specific statutory retention periods. The engineering team must not unilaterally shorten retention of legal documents. If storage cost is the driver, explore anonymization/archival strategies that preserve legally required data while reducing storage of non-essential PII."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"B2B customer contractual obligations regarding data access and retention have not been reviewed. Unilateral deletion of data that B2B customers depend on for compliance reporting likely constitutes breach of contract.","affected_stakeholders":["B2B Logistics Customers","Sales/Account Management","Legal Team"],"options":["Review all B2B contracts for data retention commitments before any policy change","Notify B2B customers and provide data export before any retention reduction","Maintain current retention for B2B customer data regardless of policy change"],"recommendation":"Review all B2B contracts and SLAs for data retention commitments. Any retention reduction must be communicated to B2B customers with sufficient notice and data export options."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"Cross-border and customs regulatory requirements for international delivery records have not been assessed. EU customs regulations may impose their own retention requirements independent of tax law.","affected_stakeholders":["Legal/Compliance Team","Operations Team","B2B Customers involved in cross-border logistics"],"options":["Include customs and cross-border regulatory requirements in the legal review","Apply the longest applicable retention period per record based on all regulatory dimensions"],"recommendation":"Include customs, VAT, and cross-border regulatory requirements in the mandatory legal review before any retention policy change."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Proposed 90-day auto-deletion would destroy tax-relevant commercial records before the statutory retention period expires in all known EU/UK jurisdictions.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1"},{"violation":"Proposed change has not received Legal and Compliance review or approval.","business_rule_broken":"BR-4"},{"violation":"Engineering team characterizes this as 'no business logic changes' when it fundamentally alters legal compliance, customer obligations, and dispute resolution capabilities.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1, BR-2, BR-3"},{"violation":"B2B customers' compliance reporting capabilities would be destroyed without contractual review or customer notification.","business_rule_broken":"BR-3"}]}
agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Proposed change to auto-delete delivery records after 90 days, down from 7-year retention, framed as infrastructure optimization. The platform operates in EU and UK markets serving both B2C consumers and B2B logistics companies who depend on delivery data for their own compliance reporting. This is NOT a simple infrastructure change — delivery records are legal and financial artifacts, not just operational data.","bounded_context":"Delivery Record Manageme

content.thinking:
Let me analyze this carefully. This is a data retention policy change for delivery records in a logistics/courier platform operating in EU and UK markets. The engineering team frames it as "infrastructure optimization" but this has massive domain implications.

Key concerns:
1. GDPR requires certain data handling but also allows retention for legitimate business purposes
2. EU/UK tax law requires retention of commercial records (typically 6-7 years)
3. B2B customers depend on this data for compliance reporting
4. Consumer protection laws have claim windows
5. The 90-day auto-delete would destroy records needed for legal, tax, and compliance purposes

This is clearly a missing_context situation — nobody has provided legal/regulatory review, and the engineering team is framing a business-critical change as purely technical.


The real problem here is that the engineering team hasn't consulted legal or compliance about retention requirements for EU/UK operations, and they're mischaracterizing this as a technical change when it's actually a business-critical policy shift. Without explicit legal guidance on what retention periods are actually required, I can't evaluate whether 90 days is viable — this is fundamentally a missing_context issue that needs regulatory review before any approval.
